critique.summary: The article discusses the development and testing of a 3dfx Voodoo graphics driver and glide2x port for the IRIX operating system on IP32 architecture, specifically supporting SST1 (Voodoo1) hardware. It provides technical details, logs, and links to source code repositories related to the driver and hardware detection.
mainPoints: The driver currently supports only SST1 (Voodoo1) on IP32 (O2) systems running IRIX 6.5.30 with RM7000C CPU.
Source code for the driver, glide2x IRIX port, and a hardware inventory wrapper with 3dfx support are available on GitHub.
The article includes detailed system logs showing successful initialization and detection of the 3dfx Voodoo1 graphics card.
Hardware specifications such as CPU type, memory size, and PCI device IDs are listed to confirm the environment where the driver was tested.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article are consistent with known technical details about IRIX systems, IP32 architecture, and 3dfx Voodoo hardware. The presence of GitHub repositories with source code supports the claim of active development and porting efforts. The detailed system logs and hardware information align with typical outputs from IRIX systems and 3dfx driver initialization, indicating the article is factually accurate. However, the article does not cite external sources or provide multiple perspectives, limiting its journalistic reliability.
considerations: The article does not discuss compatibility with other 3dfx hardware beyond SST1 or with other IRIX versions or hardware architectures.
There is no mention of performance benchmarks or stability testing results for the driver.
The article lacks contextual information about the significance or potential use cases of this driver port in the broader IRIX or legacy hardware community.
Security implications or support status of the driver and IRIX version are not addressed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around historical and technical aspects of IRIX, 3dfx Voodoo graphics cards, and related hardware comparisons. Commenters share personal experiences, technical insights, and humorous analogies related to vintage graphics technology.

comments.author: kmavm
comments.text:
I was an intern at SGI in the summer of 1998, when we shipped the latest minor version of IRIX, 6.5. I worked on a test suite for IRIX's pthreads implementation, and got to ship a teeny, tiny bit of real code that fixed a real-time hold-off in pthread_mutex_t. (IRIX is a hard RTOS, you see.) As things happened, the dot-dot releases of that minor version would be the last releases of IRIX to roll off the software assembly lines before SGI put it in maintenance mode for these last darn-near-30-years.

In 2000, I was the 20th-or-so full-time engineer at VMware, where I worked for 9 years. Then was at Facebook from 2009 to 2016, where I worked on the search backend (now replaced), HHVM (which still runs the Big Blue Application, a shrinking portion of the Meta Empire), and started FAIR in 2015 (which finally seems to have turned around the "open" sign with Yann's departure).

In 2016 I started at Slack as Chief Architect, where I mostly did not write a ton of code. I worked on a job queue scheduler which I would not be surprised to find has been replaced. And after that I was mostly encouraging/advising people doing Real Work.

All of which is to say, it is quite possible that the last code I've worked on professionally that is out there running on customer machines ... is that libpthread mutex bug fix from when I was barely old enough to drink.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a credible, detailed personal account with verifiable career history, adding valuable context about IRIX development and its legacy.

comments.author: somat
comments.text:
Now I am curious, how does the voodoo 1 compare with the native o2 gfx?

The o2 used a unified memory scheme so it's graphics were never as fast as it's big brother the octane's impact graphics but because of the unified memory it was a texture power house in comparison, close to a GB of texture memory in 1996 is mind blowing, in comparison the ocatane's impact graphics had 4 mb of texture memory and if you payed out the big bucks for a max impact with double the memory(which was the size of a large motherboard)... you still only got 4mb because the extra memory was basicly sli. and a graphics board that had the reputation of desoldering it's own memory off.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a technically accurate comparison of O2 and Octane graphics memory architectures, reflecting knowledgeable insight into vintage SGI hardware.

comments.author: HerbManic
comments.text: Very nice. I guess the final boss would be to port Quake 1 with glide to support this.
comments.analysis: A relevant and constructive suggestion that aligns with the topic, though it is more of an idea than a factual statement.

comments.author: cellularmitosis
comments.text:
Klipsch was famous for making speakers using acoustic horns, which was somewhat esoteric. When they finally made a speaker which used a conventional woofer, they named it “the heresy”.

A 3dfx card running in an O2 surely deserves a similar moniker :)
comments.analysis: This comment uses a humorous analogy to highlight the novelty of running a 3dfx card in an O2, adding color but less technical substance.
